original_abstract:
Characterizing new spin qubit candidates is a primary objective in the developing field of molecular spintronics and quantum information science. Recent studies have highlighted photoexcited chromophore–radical systems as particularly promising due to their potential for high-purity initialization and multi-level (qudit) logic operations. In this work, we investigate a series of three chromophore–radical dyads consisting of a perylene diimide (PDI) unit linked to a stable 2,2,6,6-tetramethyl-piperidin-1-oxyl (TEMPO) radical. Using transient electron paramagnetic resonance (EPR) spectroscopy, we examine how isotopic labeling ($^{15}$N and deuterium) of the TEMPO moiety influences the magnetic properties and spin coherence of the light-generated quartet states. 

By conducting experiments in rigid polymer matrices (PMMA), we demonstrate that electron spin coherence can be maintained even under ambient conditions. Our findings reveal that the coherence time of the photogenerated high-spin state is directly correlated with the coherence properties of the radical precursor. We report a room-temperature spin coherence time ($T_m$) of up to 0.7 µs and successfully perform Rabi oscillation experiments to demonstrate coherent spin manipulation. This study represents a significant step toward the practical implementation of organic molecular spin systems in functional quantum devices operating at room temperature.
humanized_abstract:
Photophysical properties of radicalized chromophore-polyaromatic dye complexes are currently under investigation for applications ranging from molecular spintronics to quantum information processing. While the prospects of hybrid chromophore/radical complexes such as photoexcited electron donor-acceptor (EDA) dyads based on neutral chromophores (like naphthalene derivatives, 2,1,3-Benzothiazole derivatives or substituted Pyrenes ) have been shown and widely discussed, photoexcited chromophore–radical pairs based on electron accepting dyechromophores and free radicals have recently attracted strong interest due to potential for highly efficient polarization of spin-population giving rise to highly polarized spins facilitating thus high fidelity of initialization and thereby feasible multi-level or qudit logic implementations. Herein, three chromophore–radical pairs consisted of perylene diimide (PDI) units linked to TEMPO radicals are investigated as potential qudit precursors. Their light-induced quartet excited states are characterized with transient EPR spectra and their 15N,2H labeling influences are scrutinized.

We investigated the spin properties of persistent high-spin species that are generated by direct photoexcitation of stable free radical precursors dissolved in rigid polymer matrices. All experiments were performed using photoexcited PMMA containing a stable free radical precursor. We observe electron spin coherence under ambient conditions and show that the coherence time of the photogenerated high-spin state is linked to the coherence of the precursor radical. We observe at room temperature a maximum $T_m$ value of 0.7 µs for the photogenerated high-spin state, and demonstrate Rabi oscillations for the coherence path. This proof-of-concept study is an important step towards the realization of functioning organic molecular spin based quantum devices under room temperature conditions.

document.output:

While the prospects of hybrid chromophore/radical complexes such as photoexcited electron donor-acceptor (EDA) dyads based on neutral chromophores (like naphthalene derivatives, 2,1,3-Benzothiazole derivatives or substituted Pyrenes ) have been shown and widely discussed, photoexcited chromophore–radical pairs based on electron accepting dyechromophores and free radicals have recently attracted strong interest due to potential for highly efficient polarization of spin-population giving rise to highly polarized spins facilitating thus high fidelity of initialization and thereby feasible multi-level or qudit logic implementations. Herein, three chromophore–radical pairs consisted of perylene diimide (PDI) units linked to TEMPO radicals are investigated as potential qudit precursors. Their light-induced quartet excited states are characterized with transient EPR spectra and their 15N,2H labeling influences are scrutinized.
